problem.text: Softlight's Inspiration app is a chat-based tool for product designers — they describe a design problem, optionally attach screenshots, and the AI surfaces relevant real-world design examples from sources like Figma and Mobbin to spark ideas. The logged-out landing page mirrors the authenticated experience almost exactly: same headline, same text input, same image dropzone, same example cards. But when a logged-out visitor tries to interact — clicking the image zone, clicking a header button, or (critically) submitting a query — they hit an auth modal asking them to sign in with a work Google account before they get any value. Visitors arrive without knowing whether the product is worth trusting with their work email. The page shows them the interface but gives them nothing to react to: no sample output, no sense of what a response actually looks like, no social proof, and no clear reason to create an account right now. The gap between “I see a text box” and “I understand why I should sign up” is never bridged, so most visitors leave without converting.

<slot_id>c405df03-44e6-4c2b-a241-40211bf5d398</slot_id>
<spec>
LIVE SAMPLE OUTPUT: Replace the image dropzone area with an auto-playing demo that cycles through pre-baked query/response pairs. Below the text input, show a full simulated AI response for one of the example queries — a chat bubble with the user's question, then a realistic AI text reply (2-3 sentences analyzing the design problem), followed by a static 2x2 grid of curated screenshot thumbnails with captions like 'Stripe — Progressive Disclosure' and 'Linear — Onboarding Wizard', each with a Figma or Apps source badge. The thumbnails should be placeholder-colored rectangles (#E5E0DB) with realistic caption text below them. Add a frosted-glass gradient overlay at the bottom of this sample output area with a prominent CTA button 'Sign up to get your own results' centered on it. The sample output section should sit between the form and the examples section, with a subtle left-border accent in #453321 to visually separate it as 'AI output'. Keep the existing headline, text input, and example cards. Remove the dropzone entirely.
</spec>

Run the generate-content-script skill. Read the skill file first, then follow it.

<project_id>0f9b197c-ce58-414f-943f-feef74916c48</project_id>
<slot_id>6e2a663a-dec2-413b-8ca0-bc6ca86d944f</slot_id>
<spec>
SOCIAL PROOF BANNER + COMPANY LOGOS: Add a horizontal strip below the headline reading 'Trusted by design teams at' followed by a row of 6 company logo placeholders (light gray rounded rectangles, 80x28px each, with placeholder text: 'Stripe', 'Notion', 'Figma', 'Linear', 'Vercel', 'Ramp') in #8B7F73 color, spaced evenly with 24px gap, opacity 0.6. Below the example cards section, add a testimonials row: three testimonial cards in a 3-column grid matching the example card styling (#F9F7F4 background, #efe2dc border, 16px border-radius). Each card contains an italic quote (14px, #7a6f65), a name in bold (14px, #453321), and a role/company line (12px, #8B7F73). Use these testimonials: (1) 'This completely changed how I approach early-stage design exploration.' — Sarah M., Senior Designer at a fintech startup, (2) 'I found patterns I never would have discovered on my own. Saves hours of research.' — James K., Product Designer, (3) 'The Figma integration alone makes this indispensable for our team.' — Priya R., Design Lead. Keep everything else — headline, text input, dropzone, example cards, header buttons — exactly as they are.
</spec>

Run the generate-content-script skill. Read the skill file first, then follow it.

<project_id>0f9b197c-ce58-414f-943f-feef74916c48</project_id>
<slot_id>58b23c64-a8be-4892-86ca-23cb909bfe18</slot_id>
<spec>
INTERACTIVE PREVIEW — TRY-BEFORE-SIGNUP: Transform the landing page into a split layout. Left side (55% width): keep the existing headline, text input (but reduce rows to 3), and the CTA button. Remove the dropzone and example cards from the left. Right side (45% width): show a mock chat conversation that is already populated. The mock conversation contains: (1) a user message bubble showing 'I need to design a settings page that handles both simple toggles and complex nested configurations' in the UserMessageBlock style, (2) below it, a reasoning summary line 'Analyzed 847 design patterns across 12 categories' in muted text (#8B7F73, 13px, italic), (3) then a simulated AI reply: 'Great question — nested settings UIs are tricky. Here are patterns that handle hierarchy well while keeping things scannable.' (15px, #453321), (4) then a 2x3 grid of image placeholder cards (colored rectangles in alternating soft tones: #E8E3DE, #DDD5CC, #E2DCD5) each 160px tall with rounded corners (12px), a small caption below each: 'Linear Settings', 'Notion Preferences', 'Figma Config Panel', 'Stripe Dashboard', 'Discord Settings', 'Slack Preferences'. The right panel should have a light background (#F9F7F4) with a 1px left border (#E5E2DC). At the bottom of the right panel, overlay a frosted bar with 'Sign up to explore your own results' CTA button. The entire layout uses CSS grid with a 1fr gap between columns.
</spec>

Run the generate-content-script skill. Read the skill file first, then follow it.

<spec>
VALUE PROPOSITION BLOCKS + FEATURE BREAKDOWN: Keep the existing headline at the top, but add a subtitle below it: 'Describe any design challenge. Get curated inspiration from Figma Community, real apps, and design systems — in minutes, not hours.' in 18px, #7a6f65, max-width 560px, centered, line-height 1.5. Below the text input and CTA (keep both, remove the dropzone), replace the example cards section with a 3-column feature grid. Each feature block: white background (#F9F7F4), 16px border-radius, #efe2dc border, padding 28px. Feature 1: icon area (32x32 colored circle in #453321 with a search magnifier symbol as text), title 'AI-Powered Search' (16px, bold, #453321), description 'Describe your design problem in plain language. Our AI understands context, constraints, and intent.' (14px, #7a6f65). Feature 2: icon circle, title 'Real-World Examples', description 'Get inspiration from Figma Community files and production apps — not generic stock UI kits.' Feature 3: icon circle, title 'Instant, Not Hours', description 'What used to take hours of browsing Dribbble and Mobbin now takes under 3 minutes.' Below the feature grid, add a full-width CTA bar: 'Start finding inspiration — free for designers' as a large button (width 100%, max-width 400px, centered, 48px height, #453321 background, white text, 12px border-radius).
</spec>

Run the generate-content-script skill. Read the skill file first, then follow it.

<project_id>0f9b197c-ce58-414f-943f-feef74916c48</project_id>
<slot_id>95b9eef6-cc15-46ac-b8c0-6391924b046e</slot_id>
<spec>
PROGRESSIVE DISCLOSURE — FREE PREVIEW QUERY: Keep the entire existing landing page layout exactly as-is (headline, text input, dropzone, CTA, example cards, header buttons). Change the behavior: when the user submits a query or clicks an example card, instead of immediately showing the auth modal, transition to a 'preview results' state. In this state, show the ChatPage layout with the user's message displayed, then a 'Thinking' shimmer animation for 2 seconds, then fade in a blurred results panel. The results panel shows: the AI reply text 'Here are 8 design patterns that match your problem...' (15px, #453321) clearly readable, followed by a 2x3 grid of image cards that are heavily blurred (CSS filter: blur(8px)). Each blurred card is a colored rectangle (180px tall, alternating #E8E3DE and #DDD5CC) with a blurred caption area below. Over the blurred grid, center a glassmorphism overlay card (backdrop-filter: blur(20px), background: rgba(249,247,244,0.85), border-radius: 16px, padding: 32px 48px, border: 1px solid rgba(239,226,220,0.8)). Inside: 'Sign up to reveal your results' as a heading (Merriweather, 24px, #453321), a subline 'Your personalized inspiration is ready' (14px, #8B7F73), and the Google sign-in button matching the auth modal styling. This creates a teaser that shows value was generated but requires signup to access.
</spec>

Run the generate-content-script skill. Read the skill file first, then follow it.

HERO REDESIGN — BOLD VISUAL STORYTELLING: Completely redesign the above-the-fold area. Remove the text input and dropzone from the initial view. New layout: Large hero section (full viewport height minus 48px). At the top-right, keep the Log in / Sign up buttons. Centered content: the headline 'Inspiration from unexpected places' in larger text (48px Merriweather, #453321), with a subtitle 'AI-powered design research for product teams' (18px Figtree, #7a6f65, margin-top 16px). Below the subtitle (margin-top 40px), show a large screenshot/mockup frame: a 680px wide, 400px tall rounded rectangle (border-radius: 16px, border: 1px solid #E5E2DC, background: #F9F7F4, box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(69,51,33,0.08)) containing a simulated product screenshot. Inside this frame: left portion (40%) shows a mini chat conversation (user bubble + AI reply text, small font 11px), right portion (60%) shows a 2x3 grid of small image thumbnails (placeholder colored rectangles in warm tones). Below the mockup frame (margin-top 40px), a single large CTA button: 'Try it free' (#453321 background, #f5f1ed text, 48px height, 200px min-width, 12px border-radius, 16px font-size, bold). Below the CTA (margin-top 12px), small text: 'No credit card required. Works with your team Figma files.' (13px, #8B7F73). Completely remove the example cards section and the text input from the hero view.
</spec>

<spec>
BEFORE/AFTER COMPARISON + URGENCY: Keep the headline. Below it, add a comparison section with two side-by-side panels in a 2-column grid (gap: 20px, max-width: 700px). LEFT PANEL ('Without Inspiration'): background #F9F7F4, border 1px solid #efe2dc, border-radius 16px, padding 24px. Header: 'Without Inspiration' in 14px bold #8B7F73 with a red-ish dot (8px circle, #E26262) beside it. Content: a bulleted list (14px, #7a6f65, line-height 1.7): 'Browse Dribbble for 45 min', 'Search Mobbin with vague keywords', 'Scroll through Figma Community', 'Screenshot and organize manually', 'Still not sure what patterns apply'. At the bottom: '~2 hours' in bold 20px #E26262. RIGHT PANEL ('With Inspiration'): same card styling but with a green dot (8px circle, #34A853). Content list: 'Describe your design problem', 'AI finds relevant patterns', 'See real Figma files and production apps', 'Get contextual explanations', 'Curated results you can act on'. Bottom: '~3 minutes' in bold 20px #34A853. Below the comparison, keep the existing text input (remove the dropzone), CTA button, and example cards as-is. Between the comparison and the text input, add a single line of text: 'Try it now — describe your design challenge below' (16px, #453321, text-align center, margin 32px 0).
</spec>

comments.text:
This is the strongest direction here. It directly addresses the core problem: visitors see a text box but have no idea what they would get back. By showing a realistic query/response pair with the AI analysis text and the thumbnail grid, you are answering the question "what does this product actually do?" before asking anyone to sign up. That is exactly the gap the current page fails to bridge.

The decision to use one of the existing example queries as the demo content is smart — it connects the sample output to the clickable cards below, which reinforces credibility.

Two things to push on in the next iteration: First, the simulated output currently uses placeholder rectangles for the thumbnails. In production, the actual results include real screenshots from Figma Community files and apps with source badges (Figma, Apps). The placeholder blocks look obviously fake and undermine the value demo. We should explore whether we can use a small set of real, pre-selected screenshots here — even just 4 static images that we hand-pick and serve as assets. The AI response text can be hardcoded, but the visual thumbnails need to feel real for this to land. Second, the "Sign up to get your own results" CTA is positioned well, but consider whether the frosted overlay at the bottom of the sample output is cutting off content the visitor needs to see to be convinced. The visitor should see enough output to feel the value, with the gate coming after that moment — not during it.

This is exploring the same core idea as the Live Sample Output — showing visitors what the product does before asking them to sign up — but the split layout execution has problems.

The concept of a side-by-side "input on the left, output on the right" layout is interesting because it mirrors the actual product experience (the authenticated ChatPage uses a main panel + side panel layout). That connection is good. But the current execution makes the page feel cramped and unfocused. The headline and text input are pushed to the left half of the viewport, which breaks the centered, spacious feel of the baseline. The mock conversation panel on the right is dense — user bubble, reasoning line, AI reply, then a 2x3 grid — and at 45% width it does not have enough room to breathe.

The "Analyzed 847 design patterns across 12 categories" line is a nice touch — it gives visitors a sense of the depth behind the product. Consider pulling that idea into the Live Sample Output direction.

I would not pursue this layout further as a standalone direction. But the specific details — the reasoning summary line, the volume stat — are worth combining into the stronger inline approach from the first prototype.

The concept here is compelling — let the visitor actually submit their own query, show them that the system did produce results, but blur them behind a sign-up gate. It turns the auth wall from "you cannot use this" into "your results are ready, sign up to see them." That is a much better psychological framing.

The problem is that the screenshots show the page looking identical to the baseline in its default state. The behavioral change only happens after query submission, which means the static landing page still has the same conversion problem — nothing on the page communicates value before the visitor decides to interact. This approach does not fix the first-impression problem. It only improves the moment after someone has already decided to engage.

This is not mutually exclusive with the other directions. The blurred-results gate could be combined with the Live Sample Output approach: show a pre-baked demo on the landing page (so visitors understand the product instantly), and then when they submit their own query, show blurred personalized results with a sign-up CTA. That combination would address both the first-impression gap and the post-interaction conversion moment. Worth exploring as a second phase — but the landing page itself needs to change first.

Social proof is a reasonable thing to add to a conversion-focused landing page, but this direction has a fundamental feasibility problem and does not address the core issue.

First, the feasibility problem: the company logos (Stripe, Notion, Figma, Linear, Vercel, Ramp) and the testimonial quotes are entirely fabricated. We do not have published case studies or permission to use these company names as social proof. Shipping this with fake logos would be dishonest and could create real problems. If we want social proof, we need real users who have agreed to be referenced.

Second, even if the social proof were real, this direction does not solve the main problem. The visitor still sees the same text box, the same dropzone, the same example cards. Adding a logo strip and testimonials below the fold does not bridge the gap between "I see a text box" and "I understand what this product does." It tells the visitor that other people use it, but not what they would get out of it. Social proof works as an accelerant when the value proposition is already clear — it does not substitute for showing the value.

If we ship a stronger direction (like the Live Sample Output), social proof could complement it as a secondary element. But it should not be the primary lever.

This is the most conventional landing page approach of the set — subtitle explaining the product, feature blocks with icons, prominent CTA. It is well-structured and the subtitle text ("Describe any design challenge. Get curated inspiration from Figma Community, real apps, and design systems — in minutes, not hours.") does a better job of explaining the value than the current page, which says nothing about what the product does.

But it replaces "show, don't tell" with "tell, don't show." The feature blocks describe what the product does in words — AI-Powered Search, Real-World Examples, Instant Not Hours — but none of that is as convincing as actually seeing a sample output. Visitors to a tool like this are designers. They respond to visual evidence, not feature marketing copy.

The subtitle copy is good and worth pulling into whatever direction we pursue. The feature blocks themselves feel generic — they could describe any AI search product. I would not pursue this layout further. The subtitle and the "minutes, not hours" framing are the valuable pieces here; fold them into the Live Sample Output direction.

This is visually the most polished prototype in the set. The larger headline, the subtitle, the product preview panel with the mock chat and colorful thumbnail grid, the clean CTA, the reassurance text — it all reads as a professional, confident landing page. The visual execution is strong.

But this direction makes a strategic trade-off I am not comfortable with: it removes the text input entirely from the hero. The current page has a problem — visitors see a text box but do not understand why they should use it. This direction solves that by removing the text box altogether and replacing it with a marketing page. That fixes the confusion, but it also removes the one thing that makes this landing page distinctive: the fact that it looks and feels like the actual product.

The product preview panel (chat + thumbnail grid) is doing the right thing — showing visitors what they would get. But the colorful placeholder thumbnails (warm oranges, golds) are obviously fake and feel more like a marketing illustration than a real product screenshot. Compare this to the Live Sample Output direction, which shows the same kind of content but inline, in context, as part of the actual product interface. That feels more credible.

The "Try it free" CTA with "No credit card required" is clean and worth keeping. But I would rather see the product preview concept executed within the existing page structure — keep the text input, add the demo output below it — than go full marketing hero.

The before/after comparison is a well-known conversion pattern, and the specific framing here — "~2 hours" of manual browsing vs "~3 minutes" with Inspiration — does communicate the value proposition clearly. The red/green color coding with time estimates is immediately readable.

But this has the same weakness as the Value Proposition Blocks: it tells rather than shows. A designer landing on this page reads two bulleted lists comparing workflows and thinks "okay, that sounds nice." They do not think "I need to try this right now." The emotional gap between reading about time savings and seeing actual results is large.

The visual execution is clean — the two cards are well-structured and the page flows logically from comparison to CTA to text input. But the comparison panels push the text input below the fold, which means the visitor has to scroll past marketing content before they can interact with the product. That is the opposite of the current page's strength, which puts the interactive surface front and center.

The "~2 hours vs ~3 minutes" framing is sharp and worth preserving as copy somewhere on the page. But as the primary conversion strategy, comparison panels are not going to move the needle as much as actually demonstrating the product.

Overall synthesis and recommended direction for the next iteration:

The Live Sample Output (first prototype) is the clear winner and should be the direction we pursue. It is the only approach that directly shows visitors what the product does — which is the single most important thing missing from the current page.

For the next iteration, I would like to see the Live Sample Output direction refined with the best elements pulled from the other explorations:

1. Use real screenshot thumbnails instead of placeholder rectangles. The authenticated product pulls from Figma Community and Mobbin — we can hand-pick 4 compelling examples and serve them as static assets. This is the most important feasibility issue to solve.

2. Add the "Analyzed 847 design patterns across 12 categories" reasoning summary line from the Split Layout prototype. It gives visitors a sense of the system's depth.

3. Consider adding the subtitle copy from the Value Proposition Blocks ("Describe any design challenge. Get curated inspiration...") between the headline and the text input. The headline alone does not explain what the product is.

4. The "No credit card required" reassurance text from the Hero Redesign is a low-cost addition that reduces friction.

5. Keep the text input and example cards — they are distinctive and functional. Do not go full marketing page.

6. The blurred-results gate from the Progressive Disclosure prototype is a strong second-phase idea. Once the landing page itself communicates value, we could also intercept actual query submissions with a blurred preview instead of the immediate auth modal. But get the static landing page right first.

One idea I would add that none of the prototypes explored: the current page has a dropzone for uploading screenshots, but clicking it immediately triggers the auth modal. Instead of removing the dropzone entirely, we could consider keeping it but making it clearly secondary — maybe collapsed or as a small mention below the text input. Image upload is one of the product's differentiating features (most competitors do not accept visual context), and removing all trace of it from the landing page loses that signal.

Seeing this for the first time — yes. This is the right idea and the execution held up better than I expected working blind.

The PM is right that it directly solves the core gap. What I want to highlight from a craft perspective is that the inline placement works because it respects the page's existing visual rhythm. The simulated chat bubble uses the same dark brown (#453321) background and the same border-radius (16px) as the existing CTA and auth modal elements, so it reads as part of the product rather than a marketing insert. That continuity is doing a lot of heavy lifting.

The placeholder thumbnail rectangles are the biggest visual regression compared to baseline. The production example cards have a gradient fade (linear-gradient to #F9F7F4), the 1px #efe2dc border, and 16px border-radius that make them feel like real content containers. The blank gray blocks break that illusion completely. I agree with the PM — we need 4 real screenshots served as static assets. But I would go further: each thumbnail should use the same .landing-page-example-card styling (background: #F9F7F4, border: 1px solid #efe2dc, box-shadow: 0 1px 0 rgba(26,26,26,0.03), border-radius: 16px) so they look like they belong to the same component family as the example query cards below.

The source badges ('Apps', 'Figma') next to the captions are a nice detail — they give credibility by signaling where results come from. Keep those.

The 'Sign up to get your own results' CTA is positioned well but it is using a full-width dark bar that feels heavier than anything in the existing design system. I would switch it to the same button style as .landing-page-cta (background: #453321, border-radius: 12px, padding: 12px 16px) and right-align it to match the existing 'Find inspiration' button placement. Less aggressive, more consistent.

Spacing note: there is about 24px between the AI response text and the thumbnail grid. The baseline uses 32px gap between the centered content sections. Bump that to 32px for consistency.

I agree with the PM that the fabricated logos and testimonials are a dealbreaker — we cannot ship that.

But looking at this from a design execution standpoint, there are some structural pieces worth noting. The logo strip placement — sitting between the headline and the text input — actually works as a visual anchor. It breaks up what is currently a long vertical gap between the title and the form. If we ever get real social proof to work with, that is the right position for it: after the headline establishes the brand, before the input asks the visitor to do something.

The testimonial cards below the fold use the same card styling as the example cards (#F9F7F4 background, #efe2dc border, 16px radius) which is correct. But the layout feels like it is fighting for attention with the example query cards right above it. Two rows of similar-looking cards creates visual monotony. If we revisit social proof later, I would either integrate quotes as inline text between sections (no card wrapper) or use a single rotating testimonial rather than three static cards.

I would not pursue this further as a standalone direction. The PM is right. But the logo strip positioning is a design note to file for when we have real proof to show.

I want to push back on dismissing this too quickly. The PM is right that the current execution feels cramped — the 55/45 split does not give either side enough room, and the headline dropping to left-aligned breaks the centered composition that gives the baseline its calm, spacious feel. Those are real problems.

But the idea underneath is actually quite strong. This is the only prototype that tries to show the product output right at eye level, immediately visible without scrolling, sitting next to the input as a natural pair. The Live Sample Output direction puts the demo below the fold — you have to scroll past the text input and CTA to see it. This direction says: the moment you land on the page, you see both what you would type AND what you would get back. That simultaneity is powerful for comprehension.

The 'Analyzed 847 design patterns across 12 categories' line is excellent — the PM flagged it too. It gives the system weight and credibility in a single line.

The 2x3 thumbnail grid at 45% viewport width is too tight. The labels (Linear Settings, Notion Preferences, Figma Config Panel, Stripe Dashboard) are getting squeezed. But a 2x2 grid at that width would breathe fine.

I would not ship this layout as-is, but I think there is a version worth exploring: keep the centered layout from baseline, but add a contained preview panel (maybe 60% width, centered) below the headline and above the text input. Not a full split — more like a product screenshot with a subtle shadow and rounded corners, showing a condensed version of this mock conversation. It would function like the Hero Redesign's preview panel but stay inline with the centered flow instead of dominating the page.

Do not kill this idea yet. The specific details (reasoning line, volume stat, side-by-side input/output framing) are too good to lose.

I agree with the PM — this tells rather than shows, and for a design tool targeting designers, that is the wrong move. Designers scan visually before they read copy.

That said, looking at my own work here, the visual execution is actually quite tight. The three feature cards maintain the existing card style (#F9F7F4, #efe2dc border, 16px radius) and the icon circles use a brown tone that sits well in the warm palette. The subtitle text is well-written and does a better job of explaining the product than anything on the current page. The dark CTA button ('Start finding inspiration — free for designers') uses the right background (#453321) and is large enough to be the clear primary action.

But the PM nailed it: these feature blocks are generic. 'AI-Powered Search' could be any product. The icon-plus-description card is a pattern visitors have seen on a thousand SaaS landing pages and have learned to ignore.

The subtitle copy is the real asset here. I would pull 'Describe any design challenge. Get curated inspiration from Figma Community, real apps, and design systems — in minutes, not hours.' directly into the Live Sample Output direction, placed as a subtitle below the headline in Figtree 16px weight-500, color #8B7F73. It bridges the gap between the poetic headline and the functional text input.

The feature blocks themselves are a dead end. Do not iterate on them.

The PM is right that this does not fix the first-impression problem — the screenshots confirm it looks identical to baseline in its default state. As a standalone landing page change, this is insufficient.

But I want to strongly advocate for this as a Phase 2 addition to whatever we ship. The psychological reframe from 'you cannot use this' to 'your results are ready, sign up to see them' is a genuinely different conversion moment. The current auth modal (AuthModal.tsx) fires after a 1-second timeout with canDismiss={false} — it is abrupt and feels like a wall. A blurred preview would transform that into a teaser.

From a design perspective, the blur implementation matters a lot. A simple CSS filter: blur(8px) on the results container with a frosted overlay (background: rgba(245,241,237,0.7), backdrop-filter: blur(12px)) would maintain the warm beige tone of the page while making the content tantalizingly visible. The sign-up CTA should sit centered on the overlay, using the primary button style (.landing-page-cta), with copy like 'Sign up to see your results' — direct and specific to what they will get.

The key design decision: how much content to show before the blur. I would show the full AI response text unblurred, then blur the thumbnail grid. The text establishes the quality of the analysis; the blurred thumbnails create the desire to see the actual examples. That split gives enough value to convince while gating the visual payoff.

File this for Phase 2 implementation alongside the Live Sample Output landing page change.

I disagree with the PM on how quickly this should be dismissed. Looking at this as the designer who built it — this is the most visually polished thing in the set and I think the PM undervalued the product preview panel concept because of the placeholder thumbnails.

Let me be specific about what is working. The preview panel has a clean split: chat conversation on the left, thumbnail grid on the right, all contained in a single card with subtle shadow and rounded corners. That containment is doing something important — it frames the product output as a cohesive unit, a single artifact the visitor can parse in one glance. The Live Sample Output direction shows the same content but inline, which means the visitor has to scroll and mentally assemble the pieces. This direction shows it as a composed screenshot-like preview.

The PM is right that the colorful placeholder thumbnails (warm oranges, golds, dark olive) feel like a marketing illustration. But look at the structure, not the fills. The 2x3 grid with clean labels underneath each thumbnail is actually a more faithful representation of how the real product displays results than the 2x2 grid in the Live Sample Output. With real screenshots, this would look like an actual product, not a mockup.

The 'Try it free' button is wide (roughly 200px), centered, uses #453321 background — it is clean and confident. The 'No credit card required. Works with your team Figma files.' reassurance text is exactly the right thing to say. Both should carry forward.

Where the PM is correct: removing the text input entirely is a mistake. The text input is what makes this page feel like a product rather than a brochure. I would bring the text input back below the preview panel, creating a flow of: headline > subtitle > product preview > text input > CTA > example cards. The preview shows what you get; the input invites you to try it yourself.

The subtitle 'AI-powered design research for product teams' is weaker than the one from the Value Proposition Blocks. Swap it.

This direction deserves another iteration with the text input restored and real thumbnails. Do not kill it.

The PM's read on this is fair — it tells rather than shows, and the comparison panels push the text input below the fold. I agree that as the primary conversion strategy, this is not strong enough.

Looking at the visual execution: the two comparison cards are well-built. They use the existing card styling (#F9F7F4, #efe2dc border, 16px radius) and the content is cleanly structured — dot indicator, title, bulleted list, time estimate. The red (#E26262-ish) and green color coding for the time estimates is immediately readable. The typography within the cards follows the design system: 14px body text, 16px for the time callouts.

But there is a visual hierarchy problem. The two comparison cards are the same size, same weight, same visual treatment. The 'Without Inspiration' card gets as much visual real estate as the 'With Inspiration' card. In a before/after, you want the 'after' to win the comparison visually, not just textually. The 'With' card should feel lighter, more inviting — maybe a slightly elevated shadow, or a subtle green-tinted left border, something that makes the eye prefer it.

The 'Try it now — describe your design challenge below' bridging text is good transitional copy. It connects the value proposition to the action. Worth pulling into other directions.

The '~2 hours vs ~3 minutes' framing is the sharpest piece of copy in the entire prototype set. I would find a way to work it into the Live Sample Output direction — maybe as a small annotation near the AI response, like 'Found in ~3 minutes' in #8B7F73 italic, reinforcing the speed value right next to the proof of quality.

Cross-cutting designer synthesis:

I largely agree with the PM that the Live Sample Output is the strongest direction. But I want to register two disagreements before we move forward.

First, the Hero Redesign deserves another iteration. The PM dismissed it because it removes the text input, but that is a fixable problem — bring the text input back below the preview panel. The contained preview panel (chat + thumbnail grid in a single card) is a compositionally stronger way to show the product than the inline approach. It reads as a single coherent artifact rather than scattered elements. I would like to see a version that combines the Hero Redesign's preview panel with the Live Sample Output's inline placement — essentially, a contained product screenshot card sitting between the headline/subtitle and the text input. This is the direction I am most excited about as a designer.

Second, the Split Layout prototype has ideas the PM acknowledged but then consigned to 'fold into the other direction.' I think the simultaneity of input and output at the same viewport position is a genuinely different cognitive experience from scrolling to see a demo. Before we commit to the inline approach, I want to explore one more variant: a centered layout where a compact preview panel (not a full 45% split, but maybe a 500px-wide contained card) sits at the right edge of the centered content area, overlapping slightly with the form section. Something that hints at output without requiring a full layout change.

On the PM's synthesis recommendations, I agree with all of them and would add specifics:

1. Real thumbnails: use the existing .landing-page-example-card styling for the thumbnail containers. Each should be 160x120px with 12px border-radius, 1px solid #efe2dc border, and a subtle shadow matching box-shadow: 0 1px 0 rgba(26,26,26,0.03). Source badges should use an 8px border-radius pill with #F9F7F4 background and #8B7F73 text.

2. The subtitle should be Figtree 16px weight-500, color #8B7F73, centered, with 16px margin below the headline and 32px margin above the text input. Use the Value Proposition Blocks copy.

3. 'No credit card required' text: Figtree 14px weight-500, color #8B7F73, centered below the CTA with 12px margin-top.

4. The dropzone question: I agree with the PM that image upload is a differentiator worth signaling. I would collapse it to a single line below the text input — an inline link-style element: 'You can also attach screenshots' with the ImageIcon at 14px, in #8B7F73, no border, no dropzone chrome. Clicking it expands the full dropzone. This preserves the signal without the visual weight.

5. One thing nobody mentioned: the current header buttons ('Log in' and 'Sign up') are visually identical — same background, same border, same padding. The 'Sign up' button should be differentiated. Give it background: #453321, color: #f5f1ed to match the CTA button, making it the clear primary action in the header. The 'Log in' button stays as-is as the secondary action.
